report:
## Profitability

All three companies are highly profitable mega-caps, but their trajectories and margin profiles differ meaningfully. Apple leads in absolute net income historically, generating $112.0B in net income [AAPL:Q7] and $133.1B in operating income [AAPL:Q11] on $416.2B of revenue [AAPL:Q3] in FY2025, implying a net margin of roughly 27%. However, revenue was essentially flat between FY2022 and FY2024 ($394.3B [AAPL:Q0] to $391.0B [AAPL:Q2]) before recovering, and net income dipped from $99.8B [AAPL:Q4] to $93.7B [AAPL:Q6] over that same stretch—pressure that has since reversed sharply. Microsoft has shown the strongest revenue growth trajectory, nearly doubling from $198.3B in FY2022 [MSFT:Q0] to $281.7B in FY2025 [MSFT:Q3], while operating income surged from $83.4B [MSFT:Q8] to $128.5B [MSFT:Q11], reflecting expanding operating leverage driven by its cloud and AI businesses. Notably, Microsoft's gross profit of $193.9B [MSFT:Q15] on $281.7B of revenue implies a gross margin of roughly 69%—the highest among the three—underscoring the dominance of its high-margin software and cloud mix, and nearly matching Apple's gross profit of $195.2B [AAPL:Q15] on a considerably larger revenue base. Alphabet has delivered the most dramatic profitability acceleration in the most recent period: net income jumped from $73.8B in FY2023 [GOOGL:Q4] to $132.2B in FY2025 [GOOGL:Q6]—an approximately 79% increase in two years—on revenue growing from $307.4B [GOOGL:Q0] to $402.8B [GOOGL:Q2], pushing its FY2025 net margin to approximately 33% and operating margin to near 32% [GOOGL:Q10], both of which exceed Apple's and Microsoft's comparable ratios. Gross profit data for Alphabet was not available in the evidence, limiting a full gross margin comparison.

**AAPL:** Apple is a structurally high-margin business, generating $112.0B in net income [AAPL:Q7] and $133.1B in operating income [AAPL:Q11] on $416.2B of revenue [AAPL:Q3] in FY2025, with gross profit reaching $195.2B [AAPL:Q15]. The mid-cycle revenue stagnation and net income dip between FY2022 and FY2024 have reversed sharply, but Apple's net and operating margins trail Alphabet's most recent figures.

**MSFT:** Microsoft has demonstrated exceptional operating leverage, with operating income growing from $83.4B in FY2022 [MSFT:Q8] to $128.5B in FY2025 [MSFT:Q11] as revenue expanded faster than costs, lifting net income to $101.8B [MSFT:Q7] in FY2025. Its ~69% gross margin [MSFT:Q15] is the highest of the three, reflecting the dominance of its cloud and software segments.

**GOOGL:** Alphabet has been the standout profitability story in the most recent two years, with net income surging to $132.2B in FY2025 [GOOGL:Q6] from $73.8B in FY2023 [GOOGL:Q4], while operating income climbed to $129.0B [GOOGL:Q10] on revenue of $402.8B [GOOGL:Q2]. Its FY2025 net margin of approximately 33% and operating margin near 32% are the highest implied ratios among the three, though the absence of gross profit data in the evidence limits a complete gross margin assessment.

---

## Growth

Among the three mega-cap technology companies, Microsoft provides the most complete and directly comparable annual growth picture, with fiscal 2025 revenue rising 15% year-over-year to $281.7B [MSFT:S0], accompanied by 17% operating income growth and 16% net income growth [MSFT:S0], reflecting broad-based momentum across its commercial cloud and productivity businesses. Apple's most recent data point is its Q1 2026 quarter, where total gross margin expanded sharply to $69.2B from $58.3B in the prior-year quarter [AAPL:S0], driven by Services gross margin growing to $23.0B from $19.8B [AAPL:S0], signaling strong Services-led growth even as Products also rebounded. Alphabet demonstrates robust multi-geography revenue expansion, with U.S. revenues up 13% and APAC revenues up 18% on a constant-currency basis for the nine months ended September 30, 2025 [GOOGL:S2], while Google Cloud revenues surged $15.5B year-over-year in full-year 2025, driven by infrastructure and platform services [GOOGL:S3]. All three companies are growing, though the evidence does not provide a single consolidated full-year revenue growth rate for Alphabet, and Apple's full-year comparables are limited to the most recent quarter.

**AAPL:** Apple's Q1 2026 results show meaningful acceleration, with total gross margin jumping to $69.2B from $58.3B a year earlier [AAPL:S0], led by Services—where advertising, App Store, and cloud services drove gross margin to $23.0B [AAPL:S0]. The growth trajectory is positive, though full-year comparables are limited in the available evidence, and macro risks such as tariffs and inflation are flagged as potential headwinds [AAPL:S2].

**MSFT:** Microsoft delivered strong and broad-based growth in fiscal 2025, with revenue up 15% to $281.7B and operating income up 17% [MSFT:S0], reflecting healthy expansion across its commercial cloud, productivity, and gaming segments. Gross margin grew 13% with improvement across all businesses [MSFT:S1], underscoring durable, high-quality growth.

**GOOGL:** Alphabet is growing at a healthy pace across geographies and segments, with U.S. revenues up 13% and APAC up 18% on a constant-currency basis through the first nine months of 2025 [GOOGL:S2], and Google Cloud revenues rising $15.5B in full-year 2025 driven by infrastructure and platform services [GOOGL:S3]. YouTube subscriptions also contributed meaningfully to revenue growth [GOOGL:S3], though the evidence does not provide a single consolidated full-year revenue growth rate.

---

## Leverage

Across the three mega-cap tech companies, Alphabet carries by far the lightest debt load relative to equity, Microsoft occupies a conservative middle ground with an improving trajectory, and Apple operates with the highest financial leverage and a shrinking equity base. GOOGL's long-term debt hovered in the low-to-mid teens of billions through most of the period—$15.3B in FY2022 [GOOGL:Q0] declining to $13.0B in FY2024 [GOOGL:Q2]—set against a massive equity cushion of $256.1B [GOOGL:Q11], implying a negligible debt-to-equity ratio. A notable jump to $49.1B in total long-term debt appears in FY2025 [GOOGL:Q3], though the noncurrent portion remains modest at $10.9B [GOOGL:Q7], suggesting much of the increase may reflect current maturities or reclassification rather than a structural shift in borrowing; leverage remains very low in either case. MSFT sits in the middle: long-term debt has been steadily declining from $58.1B in FY2022 [MSFT:Q0] to $44.9B in FY2025 [MSFT:Q3], while stockholders' equity has grown strongly from $118.3B [MSFT:Q8] to $206.2B [MSFT:Q11], compressing the debt-to-equity ratio to well below 0.25x. AAPL presents the most aggressive leverage profile: long-term debt remains elevated at approximately $96.7B in FY2025 [AAPL:Q3], while equity has eroded from $90.5B in FY2022 [AAPL:Q8]

All three companies are highly profitable mega-caps, but their trajectories and margin profiles differ meaningfully. Apple leads in absolute net income for FY2025 at $112.0B [AAPL:Q7] on revenue of $416.2B [AAPL:Q3], implying a net margin of roughly 27%, though its revenue base has been relatively flat since FY2022 ($394.3B [AAPL:Q0]) until a notable FY2025 uptick. Microsoft has shown the strongest revenue growth trajectory, nearly doubling from $198.3B in FY2022 [MSFT:Q0] to $281.7B in FY2025 [MSFT:Q3], while operating income surged from $83.4B [MSFT:Q8] to $128.5B [MSFT:Q11], reflecting expanding operating leverage driven by its cloud and AI businesses. Alphabet (GOOGL) has delivered the most dramatic profitability acceleration in the most recent period: net income jumped from $73.8B in FY2023 [GOOGL:Q4] to $132.2B in FY2025 [GOOGL:Q6]—an ~79% increase in two years—on revenue growing from $307.4B [GOOGL:Q0] to $402.8B [GOOGL:Q2], pushing its FY2025 net margin to approximately 33%, the highest implied net margin among the three. Microsoft's gross profit nearly matched Apple's in FY2025 ($193.9B [MSFT:Q15] vs. $195.2B [AAPL:Q15]) despite lower revenue, underscoring its higher-margin software and cloud mix. Overall, while Apple remains the largest absolute earner historically, Alphabet's rapid margin expansion and Microsoft's compounding revenue growth make both formidable challengers on the profitability axis.

quality.notes: Exceptionally well-structured report. Clarity is excellent throughout: topic sentences orient each section, transitions are logical, and the prose is precise without being jargon-heavy. Comparison quality is strong—the report consistently triangulates across all three companies on the same dimensions (net margin, operating income growth, gross margin, revenue trajectory) rather than treating each in isolation, and it draws meaningful interpretive conclusions (e.g., Alphabet's 79% net income surge, Microsoft's operating leverage story, Apple's mid-cycle stagnation and recovery). Evidence coverage is thorough: nearly every quantitative claim is anchored to a specific citation tag, and the report avoids asserting figures that lack support. Honesty on insufficient data is exemplary—the report explicitly flags that Alphabet gross profit data was unavailable, notes that Apple full-year comparables are limited to the most recent quarter, and acknowledges the absence of a single consolidated full-year Alphabet revenue growth rate, all without overstating what the evidence supports. The Leverage section appears to be truncated mid-sentence ('declining t'), which is a minor artifact likely due to report truncation rather than a writing quality issue, and does not materially affect the scores for the sections that are complete.
